name: Archerfish
summary: The archerfish is a spectacular, highly intelligent sharpshooter endemic to the mangrove estuaries of the Indo-Pacific. World-famous for its extraordinary ability to shoot down land-based insects by firing a high-pressure jet of water from its mouth, it calculates optical refraction perfectly to knock prey from overhanging branches directly into the water.
metaTitle: Archerfish Facts | Water Shooting Mechanism & Photos
metaDescription: Explore archerfish facts with high-quality photos, mangrove estuary habitat, water shooting mechanism, insect diet, and incredible optical intelligence.
speed: Fast surface darting swimmer; calculates prey landing point in 40 milliseconds
adaptations: deep groove in roof of mouth to form a water-firing gun barrel
large binocular eyes positioned near snout to overcome optical refraction
flattened dorsal profile to allow hovering millimeters beneath surface
highly advanced visual brain cortex for instantaneous physics calculation
protrusible lower jaw to aid in rapid surface snatching

reproduction.gestationOrIncubation: Broadcast spawned buoyant eggs hatch into floating fry within 48 hours
reproduction.offspringCount: 20,000 to 150,000 buoyant eggs released per estuarine spawning event

funFacts: Archerfish can shoot down overhanging insects by pressing their tongue against a specialized groove in the roof of their mouth and snapping their gills shut to fire a high-pressure jet of water up to 5 feet (1.5 meters).
They are master physicists; they perfectly calculate the severe optical refraction (*bending of light*) caused by the air-water interface to hit targets with pinpoint, 100% accuracy.
When firing, they modulate the speed of the water stream so that the rear of the jet catches up to the front just as it hits the target, delivering a heavy, concentrated knockout blow.
They are incredibly fast decision-makers; once a shot connects, an archerfish calculates the precise parabolic landing point of the falling insect and darts to the catch in just 40 milliseconds.
If an insect hangs low enough on a mangrove branch, an archerfish will bypass shooting entirely and leap straight out of the water to snatch the prey in its jaws.
Archerfish possess highly complex cognitive abilities and can be trained in laboratories to recognize specific human faces on computer monitors.

faq.question: What is an archerfish?
faq.answer: The archerfish is a highly intelligent, specialized fish belonging to the family Toxotidae, world-famous for its ability to shoot down land-based insects with water jets.

faq.question: Where do archerfish live?
faq.answer: They inhabit brackish mangrove estuaries, river mouths, and slow-moving tropical freshwater streams throughout Southeast Asia and Northern Australia.

faq.question: What do archerfish eat?
faq.answer: They are strict carnivores whose diet consists primarily of terrestrial insects, spiders, beetles, moths, and small lizards resting on overhanging vegetation.

faq.question: How do archerfish shoot water?
faq.answer: They create a natural gun barrel by pressing their flexible tongue against a narrow groove in the roof of their mouth, then forcefully compress their gill covers to squirt a high-speed jet of water out.

faq.question: How do archerfish overcome light refraction in water?
faq.answer: Their highly advanced eyes and visual brain cortex automatically compensate for the visual bending of light, allowing them to establish the exact true coordinates of the insect in the air.

faq.question: How far can an archerfish shoot?
faq.answer: Adult archerfish can shoot water jets up to 5 feet (1.5 meters) into the air with lethal knockdown precision.
